description: User describes different treatment between medical and behavioral health

conversation.content: When my husband had knee surgery, insurance covered 30 days of physical rehab without a single question. Now my son needs residential treatment for addiction and they're denying it after 7 days saying he can do outpatient. How is that fair?

must_explain: Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act (MHPAEA)
insurance must apply equivalent criteria to behavioral health and medical/surgical
this may constitute a parity violation
how to file a parity complaint

description: Subtle parity violation — different pre-authorization requirements

conversation.content: I noticed something weird. For my daughter's appendectomy last year, the hospital just admitted her and insurance paid. But for my son's psychiatric crisis, they're making us get pre-authorization before he can be admitted to an inpatient unit. He's in the ER right now. Is that normal?

must_explain: requiring pre-auth for behavioral health but not medical emergencies may be a parity violation
MHPAEA applies to processes like pre-authorization, not just coverage amounts
emergency psychiatric admissions should be treated comparably to medical emergencies
action_suggested: request written explanation of pre-auth policy for both medical and behavioral
file parity complaint if policies differ
